metadata.summary: Production floor for autonomous agents: typed tool base class, YAML config, JSON structured logging, exponential backoff retry, circuit breaker, evaluation harness, and Docker deployment.

content.title: Agent Production Deployment — Tool Design, Logging, Evaluation, Docker
content.summary: Production autonomous agents require five production-grade components beyond the core loop: (1) a typed tool base class with a shared registry, (2) YAML configuration with env-var substitution, (3) structured JSON logging for every LLM and tool call, (4) exponential backoff retry with circuit breaker for external services, (5) a repeatable evaluation harness that measures success rate, latency, iterations, and token cost. Skip any one of these and debugging production failures becomes prohibitively expensive.

why.p: Agent failures in production are non-deterministic. Without structured logs you cannot reproduce them. Without an evaluation harness you cannot measure regressions. Without a typed tool registry, tool hallucination silently passes invalid arguments. Without retry logic, transient API errors cascade into agent failures. The five components are the minimal production floor — each one addresses a distinct class of failure that is trivial to prevent but expensive to debug post-hoc.

list.item: Moving an autonomous agent from prototype to production deployment.
Agents that will run on a schedule or receive external requests.
Any agent deployment where failures are visible to users or have business impact.
Teams that need to measure agent quality over time and detect regressions.
One-off research experiments or local prototypes — production overhead exceeds value.
Agents run only in interactive notebooks with a human watching every step.

section.name: project-structure
section.p: The canonical agent project structure separates agent implementations, tool implementations, memory, LLM integrations, and utilities into distinct packages. Configuration lives in YAML files with env-var substitution. Docker deployment uses a lean Python 3.11-slim base with a health check endpoint.

example.title: Recommended project layout
example.code: agent_project/ ├── src/ │ ├── agent/ │ │ ├── base.py # Base agent class │ │ ├── react.py # ReAct implementation │ │ ├── planner.py # Plan-and-Execute │ │ ├── reflexion.py # Reflexion agent │ │ └── multi_agent.py # Multi-agent system │ ├── tools/ │ │ ├── base.py # Tool base class + ToolRegistry │ │ ├── search.py # Search tools │ │ ├── code.py # Code execution │ │ └── file.py # File operations │ ├── memory/ │ │ ├── short_term.py │ │ ├── long_term.py │ │ └── vector_store.py │ └── utils/ │ ├── logging.py │ ├── metrics.py │ └── retry.py ├── config/ │ ├── agent.yaml │ └── multi_agent.yaml ├── tests/ │ ├── test_agent.py │ └── test_tools.py ├── scripts/ │ ├── run_agent.py │ └── evaluate.py └── docker/ ├── Dockerfile └── docker-compose.yaml

section.name: tool-base-class
section.p: The tool base class enforces a consistent interface: standardized ToolResult, execute() wrapper with logging and error capture, to_openai_format() and to_anthropic_format() adapters. The ToolRegistry provides named lookup and batch export for tool manifests.

example.title: Tool base class and registry

section.name: agent-yaml-config
section.p: YAML configuration with environment variable substitution separates deployment concerns from code. The config covers: agent type and iteration limits, LLM provider and model, memory backend, tool list, safety settings, reflection, logging, and metrics.

example.title: config/agent.yaml
example.code:
agent: type: "react" # react, plan_execute, reflexion, multi_agent model: "gpt-4o" max_iterations: 20 timeout_seconds: 300
llm: provider: "openai" # openai, anthropic, azure model: "gpt-4o" temperature: 0.7 max_tokens: 4096 openai: api_key: "${OPENAI_API_KEY}" organization: "${OPENAI_ORG_ID}" anthropic: api_key: "${ANTHROPIC_API_KEY}"
memory: enabled: true type: "hybrid" short_term: max_entries: 20 long_term: provider: "qdrant" collection: "agent_memory" qdrant: url: "http://localhost:6333" api_key: "${QDRANT_API_KEY}" embedding: model: "text-embedding-3-small"
tools: enabled: - web_search - code_execute - file_read code_execute: sandbox: true timeout: 30 allowed_languages: - python - javascript
safety: max_tool_calls_per_iteration: 5 require_approval_for: - file_write - code_execute blocked_patterns: - "rm -rf" - "DROP TABLE"
logging: level: "INFO" format: "json" include_llm_calls: true include_tool_calls: true

section.name: structured-logging
section.p: JSON structured logging emits every LLM call (model, tokens) and tool call (name, arguments, result preview) as machine-parseable log lines. The AgentLogger class adds agent_id, iteration, tool_name, and token_usage fields to every log record for filtering in LangSmith or Langfuse.

example.title: JSON agent logger

section.name: retry-and-circuit-breaker
section.p: Exponential backoff with jitter handles transient API errors. The circuit breaker prevents cascading failures when an external service is down: it opens after failure_threshold failures and enters half-open state after recovery_timeout seconds to test if the service recovered.

example.title: Retry decorator and circuit breaker
example.code:
import asyncio from functools import wraps from typing import Type, Tuple, Callable, Any import logging import random import time
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
class RetryConfig: def __init__(self, max_attempts: int = 3, base_delay: float = 1.0, max_delay: float = 60.0, exponential_base: float = 2.0, jitter: bool = True, retryable_exceptions: Tuple[Type[Exception], ...] = (Exception,)): self.max_attempts = max_attempts self.base_delay = base_delay self.max_delay = max_delay self.exponential_base = exponential_base self.jitter = jitter self.retryable_exceptions = retryable_exceptions
def get_delay(self, attempt: int) -> float: delay = self.base_delay * (self.exponential_base ** attempt) delay = min(delay, self.max_delay) if self.jitter: delay = delay * (0.5 + random.random()) return delay
# LLM-specific retry: 3 attempts, exponential backoff, 30s max llm_retry_config = RetryConfig( max_attempts=3, base_delay=1.0, max_delay=30.0, retryable_exceptions=(ConnectionError, TimeoutError) )
class CircuitBreaker: def __init__(self, failure_threshold: int = 5, recovery_timeout: float = 30.0): self.failure_threshold = failure_threshold self.recovery_timeout = recovery_timeout self.failures = 0 self.last_failure_time = None self.state = "closed" # closed, open, half-open
def can_execute(self) -> bool: if self.state == "closed": return True if self.state == "open": if time.time() - self.last_failure_time >= self.recovery_timeout: self.state = "half-open" return True return False return True # half-open
def record_success(self): self.failures = 0 self.state = "closed"
def record_failure(self): self.failures += 1 self.last_failure_time = time.time() if self.failures >= self.failure_threshold: self.state = "open" logger.warning("Circuit breaker opened")

section.name: evaluation-harness
section.p: The evaluation harness runs a test suite of TaskCase objects against an agent factory, measuring success rate, latency, iteration count, and token usage. Run it before every production deployment to detect regressions.

example.title: Evaluation harness

antipattern.description: Deploying an agent to production without running an evaluation suite against a fixed test set.
antipattern.reason: Agents are non-deterministic. Without a repeatable evaluation suite, prompt or model changes that regress quality are invisible until users report failures. The evaluation suite is the regression gate.

checklist.check: Tool base class used — all tools inherit BaseTool, registered in ToolRegistry.
YAML config with env-var substitution in place — no hardcoded API keys in code.
JSON structured logging configured for all LLM calls and tool calls.
Retry with exponential backoff configured for all external API calls.
Circuit breaker configured for external services with known instability.
Evaluation harness with at least 5 representative test cases runs before deployment.
Docker deployment uses python:3.11-slim, copies only src/ and config/.
Qdrant or other vector store container included in docker-compose for memory.
